text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 18 October 2015 

Dr Sarah Thompson 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
St. Mary's Hospital, London  

Dear Dr Sarah Thompson, 

Mr Alan Peters, DOB: 22-03-1987, Hospital No: A739204 NHS No. 9038475621  
Flat 12, 45 Brook Lane, London, W6 9AB  

Diagnosis: 

Generalised epilepsy with previous absence and generalised tonic–clonic seizures; currently stable on valproate with adjunctive topiramate; prior trial of levetiracetam led to mood irritability and was discontinued. 

Aetiology: 

Idiopathic generalised epilepsy phenotype; normal perinatal history and no structural abnormality on MRI (2014). Family history notable for a sibling with childhood absence epilepsy. 

Other Medical Problems: 

Migraine without aura; mild vitamin D insufficiency (on supplementation). No known drug allergies. 

Seizure and Symptom Course: 

Following two episodes of dizziness and subsequent headaches, his symptoms improved with supportive care. On 17-Sep the absences diminished after dose escalation. His last episode was recorded on 25-Sep and he has remained well since. Considering that he has now been free of seizures for two weeks and his overall condition is stable, no immediate action is necessary. The patient also reports improved concentration at work and no further nocturnal events; he is sleeping better and denies new focal neurological symptoms. 

Management Plan: 

- Continue sodium valproate (Depakote EC) 500 mg mane, 1000 mg nocte; maintain single-brand prescribing. 
- Continue topiramate 50 mg nocte; consider cautious uptitration only if breakthrough absences recur. 
- Rescue plan unchanged: if any generalised tonic–clonic seizure lasts >5 minutes or clusters occur, seek urgent care. 
- Avoid sleep deprivation and excess alcohol; maintain regular meals to reduce migraine triggers. 
- Repeat serum valproate level and LFTs in 3 months; vitamin D recheck at the same time. 
- Routine review in six months in the general neurology clinic; earlier review if relapse. 

Clinical Notes: 

On today’s review he was alert, oriented and in no distress. Neurological examination was non-focal. There were no tremor or gait abnormalities. Weight stable. No evidence of rash or oedema. He describes occasional tingling in the fingers thought to be topiramate-related; currently mild and not functionally limiting. Work attendance has been uninterrupted for the last month. He is aware of DVLA guidance for generalised epilepsy; we discussed maintaining seizure freedom and the need to report any recurrence. 

Medication Tolerability and Interactions: 

No gastrointestinal upset with valproate; mood stable after levetiracetam cessation earlier this year. He uses simple analgesia for migraine no more than 2–3 times per month and has not required triptans recently. No over-the-counter herbal remedies. 

Additional Information: 

The patient received written advice on lifestyle measures and a seizure diary template. He has consented to email follow-up for interim concerns. Ã‚Â· Minor formatting artefacts may appear in electronic copies; please refer to the signed paper record for the definitive version. â€

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Clinic date is 18-Oct-2015. The letter states: "His last episode was recorded on 25-Sep and he has remained well since," and also that he has been seizure-free for two weeks by the time of review. Using the explicit last episode date (25-Sep) relative to the clinic date (18-Oct) gives roughly one seizure within the past month. As he has not been seizure-free for ≥6 months, we express the current frequency as 1 per month.
